raw_case:
Angiomatoid Fibrous Histiocytoma Initially Misdiagnosed as Elastofibroma Dorsi: A Case Report and Literature Review
Background and Objectives: Angiomatoid fibrous histiocytoma (AFH) is a rare soft-tissue tumor with a low-grade malignancy. It typically arises in superficial soft tissues of the extremities, head, neck and trunk in children or young adults. Because of its rare entity, it tends to be confused and misdiagnosed. Materials and Methods: A 12-year-old male presented with a painless mass located on his right upper back. The CT finding showed a 7.3 × 2.8 × 5.4 cm-sized, well-defined heterogeneous soft tissue mass in the right infrascapular area. We performed a complete excision, including the surrounding capsule. Result: The final pathology revealed an AFH of intermediate malignancy. On pathologic examination, the lesion was a 5.8 × 4.5 × 2.6 cm-sized mass with a mitotic count of 12/10 HPF, tumor necrosis of 0% and marked increased cellularity and spindle cell morphology. The immunohistochemical study showed negative for S100 and positive for SMA, focal positive for Ki-67, CD68 and positive for CD99, Desmin staining. During the five years of follow-up period, he did not show any evidence of recurrence. Conclusions: The result was satisfactory. We report a case of AFH of the back initially misdiagnosed as an elastofibroma dorsi (ED) with the review of the literature for this uncommon entity.

1. Introduction

Angiomatoid fibrous histiocytoma (AFH) is a rare soft tissue tumor with low-grade malignant potential, commonly developing in the superficial soft tissues of the extremities and trunk in young ages [1]. Its incidence is approximately 0.3% of mesenchymal tumors [2]. Most cases are indolent and painless and appear in the extremities, trunk, head and neck. Besides the histological features specifically present an outer shell of lymphoid tissue, multinodular aggregates of dendritic-like tumor cells, blood-filled spaces and abundant admixed plasma cells, whereas unusual features were also observed in minor cases. They also include clear cells, rhabdomyoblast-like cells, pulmonary edema-like patterns and tumor cell cords lying in a myxoid stroma [3,4]. Elastofibroma dorsi (ED) is another soft benign tissue tumor with great rarity, accounting for 1–2% of the chest wall tumors. It also shows a similar presentation to AFH with non-tenderness and slow-growing [5,6]. It is typically found in the area of the back, which is the lower scapular region, more specifically. Whereas ED is usually found in elderly women and manual workers [7]. We report a case of AFH on the back, which was initially misdiagnosed as ED with a review of the literature. The case also illustrates the difficulty of the diagnostic approach to AFH. An appropriate permission and informed consent for publication were obtained from the patient.

2. Case Description

A 12-year-old male presented with a painless, well-circumscribed mass located on his right upper back, which developed 9 months ago. Physical examination revealed a solid mass that became manifest on the abducting shoulder (Figure 1). The lesion was grossly rounded, of rubber-like consistency, not adhering to the skin but adhesive to deep structures. The computed tomography (CT) finding showed a 7.3 × 2.8 × 5.4 cm-sized, well-defined heterogeneous soft tissue mass in his right infra-scapular area. The mass was situated deeper than latissimus dorsi muscle and had no definite fatty component. There was neither significant lymph node enlargement in the mediastinum nor remarkable finding in the tracheobronchial tree and bony thorax observed. The initial report of differential diagnosis revealed elastofibroma dorsi (ED), vascular malformation or other benign soft tissue tumor (Figure 2). Under the impression of ED, we performed a complete excision, including the surrounding capsule. The surgical approach was started to incise the skin along with the skin crease of the infra-scapular area. The dissection was continued to the deep plane below the latissimus dorsi muscle, and the sizable mass was exposed (Figure 3). Microscopically, the lesion was encapsulated with yellowish tissue of fatty consistency. The defect following the mass excision was closed in layers with surrounding tissue repair, including muscle. The skin envelope was primarily closed with minimal tension. The procedure was smooth and uneventful. The post-operative recovery from surgery was not complicated, and the patient was discharged the next day. The final pathology confirmed an Angiomatoid fibrous histiocytoma (AFH) of intermediate malignancy. From the pathologic examination, the lesion was 5.8 × 4.5 × 2.6 cm-sized, subsequently, it found a mitotic count of 12/10 HPF, tumor necrosis of 0% and marked increased cellularity and spindle cell morphology. The following immunohistochemical study showed negative for S100 and positive for SMA, focal positive for Ki-67, CD68 and positive for CD99, Desmin staining (Figure 4). He visited the medical office every 6 months for serial clinical examinations. During the five years of follow-up period, he did not have any evidence of recurrence (Figure 5).

3. Discussion

Angiomatoid fibrous histiocytoma (AFH) is a rare soft tissue tumor of uncertain differentiation. It develops in the superficial layer of the extremities, trunk, head and neck in the first two decades of life [4,5]. AFH was initially reported as diverse types of malignant fibrous histiocytoma; however, the World Health Organization formally removed AFH as a subtype of malignant sarcoma in 2002. Instead, it was classified into the category of tumors with uncertain differentiation in 2013 [6]. According to the previous reports, it is a more likely benign tumor but still shows a low-grade of malignancy [5,6]. Despite this reclassification, the exact category of the tumor and its differentiation for AFH is not clear yet. Mostly patients rarely complain of painful mass; however, some suffer from symptomatic mass and nodules [7,8]. Pathologic consultation is essential in the differential diagnosis of AFH. The tumors are grossly well-circumscribed and enclosed by a fibrous pseudo-capsule. It occurs in a wide morphologic spectrum but consistently shows the critical features such as multi-lobular growth of round cells, spindle cells or histiocyte-like cells with low mitotic activity. Furthermore, inflammatory infiltrates containing lymphocytes and plasma cells surround it [8,9]. When considering these typical development and presentation, the case we experienced showed similar features in principle. However, minor differences like the plane of the mass that was situated, which was a bit deeper than the occasions’ in most cases. As far as we know, its non-specific presentation and radiologic findings make it more difficult to distinguish between one and another.Genetically, AFH is frequently associated with the three fusion-genes, such as EWSR1-CREB1, EWSR1-ATF1, and the FUS-ATF1 fusion gene [10,11,12]. Among them, EWSR1-CREB1 is the most found in AFH [10]. Despite the genetic analysis, some tumors sharing those genetic fusions exist, and they still cause diagnostic difficulties [11,12]. Similarly to this case, Michcik et al. also reported a case highlighting the difficulty of discovering AFH [13]. Various diagnostic modalities were employed; nevertheless, the diagnostic confirmation was not achieved. A venous malformation was only aroused, and the surgical excision was followed by. Eventually, the genetic test was performed, and the final diagnosis of AFH was revealed [13].Elastofibroma dorsi (ED) is an infrequent soft tissue benign tumor with indolent fashion. It was defined in 2002 by the World Health Organization (WHO) soft tissue’s tumors taxonomy as a benign fibroblastic/myofibroblastic tumor [14]. Unlike AFH, ED affects primarily the elderly over 55 years of age, with a mean age of 60 years. Nevertheless, some cases of ED have been described also in children. ED is found more frequently in women than men. Although ED is rare and indolent, a medical practitioner should be suspicious of the tumor when a patient complains of discomfort in response to scapula movement [15]. There are three major theories that explain etiology. The first is chronic and repetitive mechanical stresses that lead to microtrauma, which continues overproduction of elastic tissue from the stimulated fibroblasts. The second one is reactive fibromatosis and secondary degeneration of elastic fibers due to vascular insufficiency, which tends to affect elderly women. The last theory is a familial predisposition with an underlying enzymatic disorder or defect [14,15]. When considering that ED is rarely mentioned in the wide spectrum of differential diagnosis of AFH, this case illustrates the challenges associated with reaching the accurate diagnosis [10].Both Angiomatoid fibrous histiocytoma (AFH) and Elastofibroma dorsi (ED) are rare and indistinct clinical entities. Basically, it is hard to reach the certainty for the initial diagnosis with physical examination, past history, complained symptoms, or even imaging studies. However, the most pivotal imaging tool should be magnetic resonance (MR) scanning among the diverse modalities from our experience and reviewing the previous literature [14]. Nevertheless, molecular studies play a vital role in verifying angiomatoid fibrous histiocytoma (AFH); thus, immunohistochemistry demonstrates positivity for desmin, CD68, and CD99, whereas cytogenetic evaluation demonstrates that the EWSR1-CREB1 fusion gene is present in a majority of AFH cases as addressed above [16,17]. Because of the lack of a specific immunoprofile, some misdiagnosis or misclassification of the tumor may occur, and it can lead to wrong management or treatment [12]. Although most cases of AFH have shown a favorable prognosis; early suspicion is essential to establishing the correct treatment plan considering the risk of local recurrence and metastasis. Thus, it is believed that appropriate wide excision and tumor-free margin under widespread impression should be critical. In addition, follow-up treatment and close observation are equally important.Misdiagnosis originates from the rarity of prevalence and the similarity of clinical presentations. Basically, misdiagnosed lesions are frequently confused with mimicking lesions because of their non-specific symptoms and signs. Thus, some are challenged to unveil the tumor identification even though the careful inspection and examination with imaging modalities were conducted [18,19]. The occasions we should pay more attention to approach the suspecting tumors exist. For instance, the tumor developed in childhood can show atypical presentations, and it leads to a delayed diagnosis and surgical treatment. The delayed intervention cannot promise the best result. Therefore, when encountering the lesions with uncertainty in children, an early biopsy is essential to have an opportunity for a better prognosis [20]. In addition, there are the malignant lesions with some chances to be confused with another tumor. Cassalia et al. also reported the comprehensive review of misdiagnosis in skin and soft tissue cancer [21,22]. When considering the importance of accurate and early diagnosis in the treatment of malignant tumors, the high level of suspicion and an early biopsy are critical to excluding malignancy. Eventually, those mimicking tumors should require the immunohistochemistry or genetic analysis to be differentiated after surgical excision. Similarly, previous published papers about the mimicking tumors and challenges of diagnosis elucidated the need for further evaluations such as immunohistochemistry or genetic studies beyond the simple microscopic examination.

4. Conclusions

Angiomatoid fibrous histiocytoma (AFH) is an uncommon, low-grade malignant tumor. It presents non-specific findings in clinical examination and imaging studies. Especially, its presentation is similar to the clinical features of elastofibroma dorsi (ED). Thus, an imaging study is required to speculate the mass. Among the imaging modalities, MR scanning might be more valuable than others for differential diagnosis. Complete surgical excision is the therapeutic option for this tumor eventually. Above all, pathological study, immunohistochemical test, and further genetic analysis should be required to differentiate and verify the tumor after surgery.

generate_case.case_summary:
- **Patient Information:** 12-year-old male
- **Chief Complaint:** Painless mass on the right upper back
- **History of Present Illness:** A mass developed approximately 9 months prior to presentation. The lesion was painless, well-circumscribed, and solid.
- **Past Medical History:** None
- **Personal History:** None
- **Family History:** None
- **Physical Examination:** A rubber-like mass, not adherent to the skin but adhesive to deep structures, located on the right upper back.
- **Ancillary Tests:** 
  - **CT Findings:** A 7.3 × 2.8 × 5.4 cm-sized well-defined heterogeneous soft tissue mass in the infrascapular area, located deeper than the latissimus dorsi muscle without fatty components or lymph node involvement.
  - **Histopathology:** A 5.8 × 4.5 × 2.6 cm mass with mitotic count of 12/10 HPF, no tumor necrosis, increased cellularity, and spindle cell morphology.
  - **Immunohistochemistry (IHC):** Negative for S100; positive for SMA, CD99, and Desmin; focal positivity for Ki-67 and CD68.
  - **Initial Differential Diagnosis:** Elastofibroma dorsi (ED), vascular malformation, or other benign soft tissue tumor.
generate_case.differential_diagnosis:
1. **Initial Consideration: Elastofibroma dorsi (ED):**
   - Rationale: The infrascapular mass, location, and painless nature initially suggested ED, a benign tumor of the chest wall. 
   - Exclusion: ED is more common in elderly women and lacks the histopathological characteristics found in this case. The IHC profile was not supportive of ED, and mitotic activity and spindle cell morphology are not typical of ED.
   
2. **Consideration of Vascular Malformation or Other Benign Tumors:**
   - Rationale: The slow growth and well-defined characteristics of the mass raised suspicion for benign lesions, such as vascular or soft tissue tumors.
   - Exclusion: While benign entities like lipomas or hemangiomas were considered, the histological findings (e.g., spindle cell morphology, mitotic activity, encapsulation) and IHC results were inconsistent.

3. **Angiomatoid Fibrous Histiocytoma (AFH):**
   - Rationale: The spindle cell morphology, encapsulated structure, positivity on SMA, CD99, Desmin, and focal positivity for Ki-67 and CD68, along with negative S100 staining, were consistent with AFH. Its presentation in a 12-year-old with a painless, well-circumscribed mass in the trunk area aligned with known characteristics of AFH.
   - Confirmation: The pathological findings and IHC patterns led to a final diagnosis of AFH, ruling out other benign and malignant possibilities. No specific genetic studies were conducted, but this was consistent with documented cases of AFH.
generate_case.final_diagnosis: Angiomatoid Fibrous Histiocytoma (AFH) of intermediate malignancy.

- **Patient Information:** 12-year-old male
- **Chief Complaint:** Painless mass on the right upper back
- **History of Present Illness:** A mass developed approximately 9 months prior to presentation. The lesion was painless, well-circumscribed, and solid.
- **Past Medical History:** None

- **Ancillary Tests:** 
  - **CT Findings:** A 7.3 × 2.8 × 5.4 cm-sized well-defined heterogeneous soft tissue mass in the infrascapular area, located deeper than the latissimus dorsi muscle without fatty components or lymph node involvement.
  - **Histopathology:** A 5.8 × 4.5 × 2.6 cm mass with mitotic count of 12/10 HPF, no tumor necrosis, increased cellularity, and spindle cell morphology.
  - **Immunohistochemistry (IHC):** Negative for S100; positive for SMA, CD99, and Desmin; focal positivity for Ki-67 and CD68.
  - **Initial Differential Diagnosis:** Elastofibroma dorsi (ED), vascular malformation, or other benign soft tissue tumor.

results.content:
<think>
Okay, let's tackle this case. The patient is a 12-year-old male with a painless mass on his right upper back that's been there for about 9 months. The mass is described as well-circumscribed and solid. The physical exam says it's rubber-like, not attached to the skin but adherent to deep structures.

Looking at the CT scan: the mass is 7.3 x 2.8 x 5.4 cm, well-defined, heterogeneous, in the infrascapular area, deeper than the latissimus dorsi, no fatty components or lymph nodes. So, it's a soft tissue mass, not involving fat or lymph nodes. The initial differential includes elastofibroma dorsi (ED), vascular malformation, or other benign tumors.

Histopathology shows a 5.8 x 4.5 x 2.6 cm mass with a mitotic count of 12/10 HPF. Wait, that's a high mitotic count. No tumor necrosis, increased cellularity, spindle cell morphology. Immunohistochemistry is negative for S100, positive for SMA, CD99, Desmin. Focal Ki-67 and CD68.

So, first, let's think about ED. Elastofibroma dorsi is typically a benign tumor, usually in adults, located in the infrascapular area. It's often associated with the latissimus dorsi and can be asymptomatic. Histologically, it has a mixture of elastic fibers and fibroblasts. However, ED is usually not spindle cell morphology. Also, ED is typically negative for SMA and Desmin. Wait, the IHC here is positive for SMA and Desmin. That's conflicting. ED is usually negative for SMA. So maybe not ED?

Vascular malformations? But the histology here is spindle cells, not vascular structures. Also, vascular malformations might have different IHC markers. CD99 is sometimes seen in vascular tumors, but I'm not sure. But the presence of Desmin and SMA might point towards a myogenic origin.

Other benign soft tissue tumors. Let's consider leiomyoma. Leiomyomas are benign smooth muscle tumors, positive for SMA and Desmin. They can occur in various locations, including the back. But they are usually smaller and less likely to be so large. Also, the mitotic count of 12/10 HPF is high for a benign tumor. Benign leiomyomas typically have low mitotic counts.

Another possibility is a spindle cell lipoma, but that would have adipose components, which the CT didn't show. The CT was heterogeneous but no fatty components. So maybe not.

Then there's the possibility of a malignant tumor, but the initial differential was benign. However, the high mitotic count (12/10 HPF) is concerning. For a benign tumor, that's high. Maybe a low-grade sarcoma? But the immunohistochemistry is positive for SMA, Desmin, CD99. CD99 is often seen in Ewing's sarcoma, but Ewing's is usually negative for SMA and Desmin. Also, Ewing's would be more common in children, but the age here is 12, which is possible. However, the histology is spindle cells, not small round cells. So maybe not Ewing's.

Another thought: solitary fibrous tumor? But those are usually positive for CD34, which isn't mentioned here. Also, they are more common in the pleura or other sites, not typically in the back.

Wait, the IHC is positive for Desmin and SMA. That suggests a myogenic differentiation. So maybe a leiomyosarcoma? But the case was initially considered benign. However, the high mitotic count is a red flag for malignancy. Leiomyosarcoma can present with high mitotic counts. However, the absence of necrosis might be a point in favor of a benign tumor, but that's not definitive.

Alternatively, could this be a benign tumor with atypical features? Maybe a spindle cell lesion with increased mitotic activity but still benign. However, the high mitotic count (12/10 HPF) is more typical of a malignant tumor. For example, in benign leiomyomas, mitotic counts are usually <1/10 HPF. So this is way higher.

Another possibility: a myofibroma. Myofibromas are benign, but they can have higher mitotic counts. However, they are more common in infants and children, and they are usually smaller. Also, they may show CD99 positivity. But the presence of Desmin and SMA might support this. However, the size here is quite large, and the mitotic count is high. Myofibromas can be solitary or multiple, but the presentation here is a single mass.

Wait, the differential included ED, which is usually in adults. The patient is a child, so ED is less likely. So maybe the initial differential is not the best. The fact that the mass is in a location typical for ED but in a child, and the IHC is positive for SMA and Desmin, which is not typical for ED.

Putting it all together: the histology shows spindle cells, positive for SMA, Desmin, CD99. High mitotic count. No necrosis. The CT shows a deep, well-circumscribed mass. The differential includes a benign tumor but the high mitotic count suggests malignancy. However, the absence of necrosis and the presence of CD99 might point towards a benign tumor with atypical features. But I'm not sure. Another angle: CD68 is focal positive, which is a macrophage marker. Maybe a histiocytic lesion? But the other markers don't fit that.

Alternatively, could this be a hemangiopericytoma? But that's usually CD34 positive. Not mentioned here.

Wait, CD99 is positive. CD99 is a marker for Ewing's, but also for some benign tumors. However, the SMA and Desmin are more myogenic. Maybe a leiomyosarcoma. But the absence of necrosis and the age could be factors. Alternatively, a benign tumor with high mitotic count but no necrosis. But that's rare.

Given the high mitotic count, the presence of spindle cells, and the IHC profile, the most likely diagnosis is a low-grade spindle cell sarcoma, possibly a leiomyosarcoma. However, the initial differential was benign, so maybe the pathologist considered it as a benign lesion with atypical features. But given the high mitotic count, it's more likely malignant. However, the absence of necrosis and the well-circumscribed nature might suggest a benign tumor with atypical features. But I'm not certain. Another possibility is a myofibroma with high mitotic count, but I'm not sure about the size.

Alternatively, the presence of CD68 might suggest some histiocytic component, but the other markers don't align. Maybe a mixed tumor?

This is tricky. The key points are the high mitotic count, which is a marker for malignancy, and the IHC markers. The combination of SMA, Desmin, and CD99 suggests a myogenic origin with some CD99 positivity. Given that, the most likely diagnosis is a spindle cell sarcoma, possibly a leiomyosarcoma. However, the absence of necrosis and the well-circumscribed borders might suggest a benign tumor. But in children, high mitotic counts are more concerning. Therefore, the final diagnosis is likely a spindle cell sarcoma, possibly a leiomyosarcoma, but the exact type would require more analysis.
</think>

### Resoning:
<step 1> The patient presents with a painless, well-circumscribed, solid mass on the right upper back, located in the infrascapular region. The lesion has been present for 9 months and is not associated with pain or other systemic symptoms. The physical examination and imaging (CT) suggest a deep-seated, heterogeneous soft tissue mass without fatty components or lymphadenopathy, which aligns with a benign or low-grade malignant process. However, the histopathological findings, including a high mitotic count (12/10 HPF), increased cellularity, and spindle cell morphology, raise concern for a more aggressive lesion.

<step 2> Immunohistochemical findings are critical for differentiation. The tumor is negative for S100, which rules out schwannoma or neurofibroma. Positive staining for SMA (smooth muscle actin) and Desmin indicates myogenic differentiation, pointing toward a smooth muscle origin. CD99 positivity is seen in various tumors, including Ewing’s sarcoma and some benign lesions, but the absence of S100 and presence of Desmin and SMA are inconsistent with Ewing’s. Focal Ki-67 and CD68 positivity suggest some proliferative activity and possible macrophage infiltration, but these are not definitive for malignancy.

<step 3> The initial differential included elastofibroma dorsi (ED), a benign lesion typically found in adults. However, ED is usually negative for SMA and Desmin, and it presents with a more fibrous, elastic histology rather than spindle cells. The location (infrascapular) is consistent with ED, but the histopathological and IHC features do not align. Vascular malformations are less likely due to the absence of vascular structures on histology and the spindle cell morphology.

<step 4> The high mitotic count (12/10 HPF) is a red flag for malignancy, as benign spindle cell tumors (e.g., leiomyoma)
